hypotheses: H1: Products predominantly associated with utilitarian attitudes at the category level support less utilitarian (and more symbolic) attitudes at the brand level. Products predominantly associated with symbolic attitudes at the category level support less symbolic (and more utilitarian) attitudes at the brand level.
H2: Although appeals matching the category-level function are more persuasive than mismatching appeals at the category level, this "matching advantage" is attenuated at the brand level.

H3: At the category level, participants with utilitarian category-level attitudes will find a utilitarian appeal more compelling than a symbolic appeal, and participants with symbolic category-level attitudes will find a symbolic appeal more compelling than a utilitarian appeal. At the brand level, these effects are attenuated.
claim.hypotheses_location: H1: page 350 under the section 'THE EFFECT OF BRANDING ON ATTITUDE FUNCTIONS' where the authors state 'We predict the following: H1...'; H2: page 352 under the section 'FUNCTION-MATCHING APPEALS AT THE BRAND AND CATEGORY LEVELS' where the authors state 'We predict the following: H2...'; H3: page 355 in the 'STUDY 3: INDIVIDUAL DIFFERENCES IN ATTITUDE FUNCTIONS' section where H3 is introduced.
claim.statement: Branding Alters Attitude Functions and Reduces the Advantage of Function-Matching Persuasive Appeals
claim.statement_location: Title and abstract (pages 348–349) and reiterated in the General Discussion (pages 358–359).

data.source: Laboratory experiments using undergraduate student participants (multiple experimental studies)
data.wave_or_subset: Multiple separate studies (Study 1A, 1B, 1C, 2A, 2B, 3, 4) each with their own samples
data.sample_size: Study 1A: N=132; Study 1B: N=206; Study 1C: N=139; Study 2A: N=117; Study 2B: N=72; Study 3: N=54; Study 4: N=102
data.notes: Data consist of experimental lab questionnaire responses (7-point Likert scales for benefit and symbolism ratings, purchase likelihood, ad evaluations). Multiple products/brands evaluated; some analyses use difference scores (benefit minus symbolism) and aggregated product-level scores for advertisements. No public dataset or code link provided in the paper.

method.description: A series of laboratory experiments where participants (primarily undergraduates) were randomly assigned to consider products at either the category (unbranded) level or brand (branded) level. Participants rated items assessing the extent to which attitudes toward products reflected utilitarian (benefit) or symbolic considerations, and in subsequent studies evaluated persuasive appeals (utilitarian vs symbolic) or real advertisements. Analyses included t-tests, mixed ANOVAs, regression analyses, and correlational analyses using difference scores (benefit minus symbolism) as measures of attitude function.
steps: Select product categories classified as predominantly utilitarian or symbolic (based on prior guidelines).
Randomly assign participants to category-level (unbranded) or brand-level (branded) conditions.
Present product/category or brand stimuli; collect ratings on 7-point scales for items assessing utilitarian (benefit) and symbolic (symbolism) aspects.
Compute difference scores (benefit minus symbolism) to index net utilitarian vs symbolic attitude function.
In persuasion studies, randomly assign participants to receive utilitarian (matching or mismatching) or symbolic appeals and collect outcome ratings (purchase likelihood, ad favorability).
Analyze data using t-tests, mixed ANOVAs for level × product/appeal interactions, and regression analyses for moderation by individual attitude function; report relevant effect sizes and p-values.
method.models: Paired and independent-sample t-tests; mixed-design ANOVAs (e.g., level × product type × appeal); linear regression analyses with interaction terms; correlational analyses (Pearson r).
method.outcome_variable: Primary outcomes: benefit rating and symbolism rating (7-point scales), composite difference score (benefit minus symbolism), purchase likelihood (1–7), advertisement evaluation/favorability (1–7).
method.independent_variables: Level (category vs brand), product type (utilitarian vs symbolic), appeal type (matching vs mismatching: utilitarian vs symbolic), brand presence (branded vs unbranded), individual difference in attitude function (difference score).
method.control_variables: Counterbalancing of product order and question order; use of multiple brand name sets in some studies to control for brand-specific effects; random assignment to conditions. No additional covariates reported.

results.summary: Across multiple experiments, branding systematically altered the attitude functions associated with products: products that are utilitarian at the category level became somewhat less utilitarian (more symbolic) when presented as brands, and products that are symbolic at the category level became less symbolic (more utilitarian) when branded. Consequently, the advantage of function-matching persuasive appeals (utilitarian appeals for utilitarian-category products, symbolic appeals for symbolic-category products) observed at the category level was attenuated or eliminated at the brand level. Results were demonstrated with both fictitious and real brands, and supported by individual-differences analyses showing matching effects at the category level but not at the brand level. Study 4's correlational analysis of real advertisements suggested that branded advertisements did not show the category-level matching advantage and in some analyses symbolic ads were evaluated more favorably for many branded utilitarian products.

numerical_results.direction: Brand × product interaction: branding made college T-shirts more utilitarian (less symbolic) and paper towels less utilitarian (more symbolic)
Category-level: unbranded paper towels rated much higher on benefits than symbolism (utilitarian)
Branding made utilitarian products more symbolic and symbolic products more utilitarian (averaged across products)
Replication: branding altered attitude functions in the same predicted directions
Matching appeals outperformed mismatching appeals at the category level but not at the brand level (interaction)
Replication: advantage of matching appeals disappeared at brand level
Individual differences moderate effect: matching advantage present at category level for individuals but attenuated at brand level

Positive correlation: for branded advertisements, higher mismatch between category function and ad function was associated with higher ad evaluations (contrary to a simple matching-advantage prediction)

metadata.original_paper_title: Branding Alters Attitude Functions and Reduces the Advantage of Function-Matching Persuasive Appeals
